output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

class TrackingFieldNode : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    explicit TrackingFieldNode(std::string name) : m_name(name) {}
    
    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_notifications++;
    }
    
    int notifications() const {
        return m_notifications;
    }
    
    void clear() {
        m_notifications = 0;
    }

private:
    std::string m_name;
    int m_notifications = 0;
};

struct FieldGraphCleanupResult {
    bool notifiedBeforeDelete;
    bool notifiedAfterDelete;
};

inline FieldGraphCleanupResult test_field_graph_cleanup(uint64_t objectId) {
    // Step 1: Create owner and field nodes
    TrackingFieldNode owner("owner");
    TrackingFieldNode field("field");
    
    // Step 2: Get observer graph instance and register nodes
    auto& observerGraph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    observerGraph.addNode(&owner);
    observerGraph.addNode(&field);
    
    // Step 3: Get field graph instance and register field under object ID
    auto& fieldGraph = reaction::FieldGraph::getInstance();
    fieldGraph.addObjField(objectId, &field);
    
    // Step 4: Bind field to owner
    owner.bind(&field);
    
    // Step 5: Trigger notification on field
    field.valueChanged(true);
    
    // Step 6: Check if owner was notified before delete
    bool notifiedBefore = (owner.notifications() > 0);
    
    // Step 7: Clear owner notification count
    owner.clear();
    
    // Step 8: Clear dependencies on owner
    observerGraph.resetNode(&owner);
    
    // Step 9: Remove field mappings for object ID
    fieldGraph.removeObjField(objectId);
    
    // Step 10: Attempt to bind field again (should fail due to cleanup)
    // This step doesn't necessarily throw, but we expect the binding to have no effect
    
    // Step 11: Trigger notification on field again
    field.valueChanged(true);
    
    // Step 12: Check if owner was notified after delete
    bool notifiedAfter = (owner.notifications() > 0);
    
    // Step 13: Close nodes
    owner.close();
    field.close();
    
    return FieldGraphCleanupResult{notifiedBefore, notifiedAfter};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/resource.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>
#include <type_traits>

namespace test_reaction {

struct VoidResourceSnapshot {
    bool retrievedIsVoid;
    bool usesSboStorage;
    bool nameAssigned;
    bool nameClearedAfterClose;
};

inline VoidResourceSnapshot test_resource_void_behavior(const std::string &nodeName) {
    // 1. Create shared Resource<Void> instance
    auto resource = std::make_shared<reaction::Resource<Void>>();
    
    // 2. Check if resource uses SBO storage
    bool uses_sbo = resource->isUsingSBO();
    
    // 3. Get value from resource
    auto value = resource->getValue();
    
    // 4. Check if retrieved value is Void type
    bool is_void = std::is_same_v<decltype(value), reaction::Void>;
    
    // 5. Get observer graph instance
    auto &observer_graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // 6. Register resource node
    observer_graph.addNode(resource);
    
    // 7. Assign node name
    observer_graph.setName(resource, nodeName);
    
    // 8. Check if name was assigned
    bool name_assigned = (observer_graph.getName(resource) == nodeName);
    
    // 9. Close node
    observer_graph.closeNode(resource);
    
    // 10. Check if name was cleared after close
    bool name_cleared = (observer_graph.getName(resource) == "");
    
    // 11. Return void resource snapshot
    return VoidResourceSnapshot{
        .retrievedIsVoid = is_void,
        .usesSboStorage = uses_sbo,
        .nameAssigned = name_assigned,
        .nameClearedAfterClose = name_cleared
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>

namespace test_reaction {

class CountingObserver : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_notifications++;
    }
    
    int notifications() const {
        return m_notifications;
    }

private:
    int m_notifications = 0;
};

struct ObserverNodeLinkSnapshot {
    int firstNotificationsAfterInitial;
    int firstNotificationsAfterUpdate;
    int secondNotificationsAfterUpdate;
    int thirdNotificationsAfterUpdate;
};

ObserverNodeLinkSnapshot test_observer_node_links() {
    // Step 1: Create CountingObserver instance
    auto countingObserver = std::make_shared<CountingObserver>();
    
    // Step 2: Create three source ObserverNode instances
    auto source1 = std::make_shared<reaction::ObserverNode>();
    auto source2 = std::make_shared<reaction::ObserverNode>();
    auto source3 = std::make_shared<reaction::ObserverNode>();
    
    // Step 3: Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Step 4: Register all nodes
    graph.addNode(countingObserver);
    graph.addNode(source1);
    graph.addNode(source2);
    graph.addNode(source3);
    
    // Step 5: Attach single observer (CountingObserver to source1)
    source1->updateObservers(countingObserver);
    
    // Step 6: Trigger notification on first source
    source1->notify();
    
    // Step 7: Get notification count after initial setup
    int firstNotificationsAfterInitial = countingObserver->notifications();
    
    // Step 8: Rewire dependencies (detach from source1 and attach to source2)
    countingObserver->updateObservers(); // Detach all observers
    source2->updateObservers(countingObserver); // Attach to source2
    
    // Step 9: Trigger notification on old source (source1)
    source1->notify();
    
    // Step 10: Get notification count after old source notification
    int firstNotificationsAfterUpdate = countingObserver->notifications();
    
    // Step 11: Trigger notification on second source
    source2->notify();
    
    // Step 12: Get notification count after second source notification
    int secondNotificationsAfterUpdate = countingObserver->notifications();
    
    // Step 13: Trigger notification on third source
    source3->notify();
    
    // Step 14: Get notification count after third source notification
    int thirdNotificationsAfterUpdate = countingObserver->notifications();
    
    // Step 15: Close all nodes
    countingObserver->close();
    source1->close();
    source2->close();
    source3->close();
    
    // Step 16: Return observer node link snapshot
    return ObserverNodeLinkSnapshot{
        firstNotificationsAfterInitial,
        firstNotificationsAfterUpdate,
        secondNotificationsAfterUpdate,
        thirdNotificationsAfterUpdate
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

   bool operator()(const NodeWeak &lhs, const NodeWeak &rhs) const {
        auto left = lhs.lock();
        auto right = rhs.lock();

        // Handle null pointers safely
        if (!left && !right) return false;
        if (!left) return true; // null comes first
        if (!right) return false;

        return left->m_depth.load(std::memory_order_relaxed) < right->m_depth.load(std::memory_order_relaxed);
    }

 So, the comparison is based on the depth of the nodes. If left's depth is less than right's, then it returns true (meaning left comes first in the ordering).

      - nullComesFirst: the result of comparing null and shallow -> true

#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/batch.h>
#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>
#include <memory>

namespace test_reaction {

struct DepthOrderingSnapshot {
    bool initialComparatorResult;
    bool shallowBeforeDeep;
    bool deepBeforeShallow;
    bool nullComesFirst;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate ObserverNode::updateDepth cooperating with BatchCompare ordering.
 *
 * @return DepthOrderingSnapshot Comparator outcomes after adjusting node depths.
 */
inline DepthOrderingSnapshot test_observer_depth_ordering() {
    // 1. Define DepthProbeNode class
    struct DepthProbeNode : reaction::ObserverNode {
        void setDepth(uint16_t depth) {
            this->updateDepth(depth);
        }
    };
    
    // 2. Get thread manager instance
    auto& tm = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    
    // 3. Reset thread manager state
    tm.resetForTesting();
    
    // 4. Enable thread safety
    tm.enableThreadSafety();
    
    // 5. Create shallow and deep DepthProbeNode instances
    auto shallowNode = std::make_shared<DepthProbeNode>();
    auto deepNode = std::make_shared<DepthProbeNode>();
    
    // 6. Create NodeWeak from nodes
    auto shallowWeak = shallowNode->getPtr();
    auto deepWeak = deepNode->getPtr();
    
    // 7. Create BatchCompare instance
    reaction::BatchCompare batchCompare;
    
    // 8. Compare initial nodes (depth 0)
    bool initialResult = batchCompare.compare(shallowWeak, deepWeak);
    
    // 9. Update depths of nodes
    shallowNode->setDepth(5);
    deepNode->setDepth(10);
    
    // 10. Compare shallow before deep
    bool shallowBeforeDeepResult = batchCompare.compare(shallowWeak, deepWeak);
    
    // 11. Update depth of shallow node again
    shallowNode->setDepth(15);
    
    // 12. Compare deep before shallow
    bool deepBeforeShallowResult = batchCompare.compare(deepWeak, shallowWeak);
    
    // 13. Create null NodeWeak
    reaction::NodeWeak nullWeak;
    
    // 14. Compare null with shallow
    bool nullComesFirstResult = batchCompare.compare(nullWeak, shallowWeak);
    
    // 15. Return depth ordering snapshot
    return DepthOrderingSnapshot{
        .initialComparatorResult = initialResult,
        .shallowBeforeDeep = shallowBeforeDeepResult,
        .deepBeforeShallow = deepBeforeShallowResult,
        .nullComesFirst = nullComesFirstResult
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <reaction/expression/expression_builders.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct UnaryExpressionSnapshot {
    int initialNegatedSum;
    int updatedNegatedSum;
    bool logicalNotAfterFlip;
    int bitwiseNotValue;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate unary expression builders and operators on reactive values.
 *
 * This function showcases how low-level expression builder helpers (`make_binary_op`,
 * `make_unary_op`) and high-level unary operators (`-`, `!`, `~`) cooperate with the
 * `expr` factory to produce reactive expressions.
 *
 * @param a First input value participating in the sum expression
 * @param b Second input value participating in the sum expression
 * @return UnaryExpressionSnapshot Snapshot of expression outputs before/after updates
 */
inline UnaryExpressionSnapshot test_unary_expression_builders(int a, int b) {
    // Create reactive variables
    auto var1 = reaction::var(a);
    auto var2 = reaction::var(b);
    auto boolVar = reaction::var(true);
    auto intVar = reaction::var(5);
    
    // Build negated sum expression
    auto sumExpr = var1 + var2;
    auto negSumExpr = -sumExpr;
    
    // Build logical NOT expression
    auto notBoolExpr = !boolVar;
    
    // Build bitwise NOT expression
    auto notIntExpr = ~intVar;
    
    // Get initial values
    int initialNegatedSum = negSumExpr.get();
    bool initialLogicalNot = notBoolExpr.get();
    int initialBitwiseNot = notIntExpr.get();
    
    // Update variables to trigger recomputation
    var1.value(a + 1);
    var2.value(b + 1);
    boolVar.value(false);
    intVar.value(10);
    
    // Get updated values
    int updatedNegatedSum = negSumExpr.get();
    bool logicalNotAfterFlip = notBoolExpr.get();
    int bitwiseNotValue = notIntExpr.get();
    
    return UnaryExpressionSnapshot{
        .initialNegatedSum = initialNegatedSum,
        .updatedNegatedSum = updatedNegatedSum,
        .logicalNotAfterFlip = logicalNotAfterFlip,
        .bitwiseNotValue = bitwiseNotValue
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

        auto and_expr = a && b;
        auto or_expr = a || b;
  - We then get the initial values of these expressions.

 However, note that the problem says: "Update variables to trigger recomputation". We are allowed to update any of the variables (a, b, c) but note that the expressions only depend on a and b. So we can update a, b, or both.

 Let's choose to update a and b to see the effect. For example, we can set a to false and b to true.

 But note: the function parameters are the initial values of a, b, c. We are going to update the reactive variables, not the parameters.

 Implementation:

 We are to return a struct with:
   initialAnd: the result of (a && b) initially
   initialOr: the result of (a || b) initially
   updatedAnd: the result of (a && b) after update
   updatedOr: the result of (a || b) after update

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct LogicalExpressionSnapshot {
    bool initialAnd;
    bool initialOr;
    bool updatedAnd;
    bool updatedOr;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate logical expression builders and operators on reactive values.
 *
 * This function shows how high-level logical operators (`&&`, `||`) compose reactive
 * expressions that are then lifted into `expr` calculations.
 *
 * @param a Initial value for the first boolean input
 * @param b Initial value for the second boolean input
 * @param c Initial value for the fallback boolean input
 * @return LogicalExpressionSnapshot Snapshot of AND/OR results before and after updates
 */
inline LogicalExpressionSnapshot test_logical_expression_builders(bool a, bool b, bool c) {
    // Create reactive boolean variables
    auto reactive_a = reaction::var(a);
    auto reactive_b = reaction::var(b);
    auto reactive_c = reaction::var(c);
    
    // Build logical expressions
    auto and_expr = reactive_a && reactive_b;
    auto or_expr = reactive_a || reactive_b;
    
    // Get initial results
    bool initial_and = and_expr.get();
    bool initial_or = or_expr.get();
    
    // Update variables to trigger recomputation
    reactive_a.value(!a);  // Flip the value of a
    reactive_b.value(!b);  // Flip the value of b
    
    // Get updated results
    bool updated_and = and_expr.get();
    bool updated_or = or_expr.get();
    
    // Return the snapshot
    return LogicalExpressionSnapshot{
        .initialAnd = initial_and,
        .initialOr = initial_or,
        .updatedAnd = updated_and,
        .updatedOr = updated_or
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction

#include <reaction/cache/graph_cache.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct NodeMetricsCacheSnapshot {

};

#pragma once

#include <reaction/cache/cache_base.h>
#include <memory>
#include <chrono>

namespace test_reaction {

struct GenericCacheStatsSnapshot {
    std::size_t entriesBefore;
    std::size_t entriesAfterInsert;
    std::size_t entriesAfterInvalidate;
    std::size_t hitCount;
    std::size_t missCount;
    double hitRatio;
    bool firstLookupWasMiss;
    bool secondLookupWasHit;
};

GenericCacheStatsSnapshot test_generic_cache_base() {
    // Step 1: Define IntCache class inheriting from CacheBase
    class IntCache : public reaction::CacheBase<int, int> {
    public:
        using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::CacheBase;
        
        // Step 2: Expose CacheBase methods using using declarations
        using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::getStats;
        using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::getCachedValue;
        using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::cacheValue;
        using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::invalidateAllEntries;
        using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::triggerCleanup;
    };
    
    // Step 3: Create IntCache instance
    IntCache cache;
    
    // Step 4: Get initial cache statistics
    auto initialStats = cache.getStats();
    std::size_t entriesBefore = initialStats.totalEntries;
    
    // Step 5: Perform initial lookup (should be miss)
    bool firstMiss = true;
    const int key1 = 42;
    const int* value1 = cache.getCachedValue(key1);
    bool firstLookupWasMiss = (value1 == nullptr);
    
    // Step 6: Insert value into cache
    cache.cacheValue(key1, 100);
    
    // Step 7: Perform second lookup (should be hit)
    const int* value2 = cache.getCachedValue(key1);
    bool secondLookupWasHit = (value2 != nullptr);
    
    // Step 8: Get cache statistics after insertion
    auto afterInsertStats = cache.getStats();
    std::size_t entriesAfterInsert = afterInsertStats.totalEntries;
    
    // Step 9: Invalidate all cache entries
    cache.invalidateAllEntries();
    
    // Step 10: Trigger cleanup
    cache.triggerCleanup();
    
    // Step 11: Get cache statistics after invalidation
    auto afterInvalidateStats = cache.getStats();
    std::size_t entriesAfterInvalidate = afterInvalidateStats.totalEntries;
    
    // Step 12: Return generic cache stats snapshot
    return GenericCacheStatsSnapshot{
        .entriesBefore = entriesBefore,
        .entriesAfterInsert = entriesAfterInsert,
        .entriesAfterInvalidate = entriesAfterInvalidate,
        .hitCount = afterInvalidateStats.hitCount,
        .missCount = afterInvalidateStats.missCount,
        .hitRatio = afterInvalidateStats.hitRatio,
        .firstLookupWasMiss = firstLookupWasMiss,
        .secondLookupWasHit = secondLookupWasHit
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<think>We are given the function signature and the structure of the test. We need to use the `reaction` library's global state functions to implement the test.

Steps:
1. Reset the global state to ensure a clean starting point.
2. Create a snapshot of the current global state (which should be the reset state) and store it in the `GlobalStateSnapshot` struct.
3. Check the state before creating any guards (this is the same as the snapshot we just took).
4. Create a `RegFunGuard` in a scope block and check the state inside that scope.
5. Create a `BatchFunGuard` in a scope block and check the state inside that scope.
6. Create a `BatchExeGuard` in a scope block and check the state inside that scope.
7. After all guards are destroyed (when the scope blocks end), reset the global state again.
8. Check the state after the guards have been destroyed and the reset.

Note: The `RegFunGuard`, `BatchFunGuard`, and `BatchExeGuard` are RAII guards that set the corresponding global state when constructed and restore the previous state when destroyed.

We are to return the `GlobalStateSnapshot` struct with the following fields:
- trackingActiveBefore: value of `isDependencyTrackingActive()` before any guards
- batchActiveBefore: value of `isBatchActive()` before any guards
- batchFunActiveBefore: value of `isBatchFunActive()` before any guards

- trackingActiveInRegGuard: value of `isDependencyTrackingActive()` inside the `RegFunGuard` scope
- batchFunActiveInBatchGuard: value of `isBatchFunActive()` inside the `BatchFunGuard` scope
- batchActiveInBatchExeGuard: value of `isBatchActive()` inside the `BatchExeGuard` scope

- trackingActiveAfter: value of `isDependencyTrackingActive()` after the guards and after reset
- batchActiveAfter: value of `isBatchActive()` after the guards and after reset
- batchFunActiveAfter: value of `isBatchFunActive()` after the guards and after reset

We must use the provided functions from the `reaction` library.

Let's write the code accordingly.
output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/concurrency/global_state.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct GlobalStateSnapshot {
    bool trackingActiveBefore;
    bool batchActiveBefore;
    bool batchFunActiveBefore;

    bool trackingActiveInRegGuard;
    bool batchFunActiveInBatchGuard;
    bool batchActiveInBatchExeGuard;

    bool trackingActiveAfter;
    bool batchActiveAfter;
    bool batchFunActiveAfter;
};

GlobalStateSnapshot test_global_state_scoped_guards() {
    // Step 1: Reset global state
    reaction::resetGlobalState();
    
    // Step 2: Create initial snapshot
    GlobalStateSnapshot snapshot;
    snapshot.trackingActiveBefore = reaction::isDependencyTrackingActive();
    snapshot.batchActiveBefore = reaction::isBatchActive();
    snapshot.batchFunActiveBefore = reaction::isBatchFunActive();
    
    // Step 3: Check state before guards (same as initial snapshot)
    
    // Step 4: Create RegFunGuard in scope block
    {
        reaction::RegFunGuard regGuard([]{});
        // Step 5: Check state inside RegFunGuard scope
        snapshot.trackingActiveInRegGuard = reaction::isDependencyTrackingActive();
    }
    
    // Step 6: Create BatchFunGuard in scope block
    {
        reaction::BatchFunGuard batchFunGuard([]{});
        // Step 7: Check state inside BatchFunGuard scope
        snapshot.batchFunActiveInBatchGuard = reaction::isBatchFunActive();
    }
    
    // Step 8: Create BatchExeGuard in scope block
    {
        reaction::BatchExeGuard batchExeGuard();
        // Step 9: Check state inside BatchExeGuard scope
        snapshot.batchActiveInBatchExeGuard = reaction::isBatchActive();
    }
    
    // Step 10: Reset global state again after guards
    reaction::resetGlobalState();
    
    // Step 11: Check state after guards
    snapshot.trackingActiveAfter = reaction::isDependencyTrackingActive();
    snapshot.batchActiveAfter = reaction::isBatchActive();
    snapshot.batchFunActiveAfter = reaction::isBatchFunActive();
    
    return snapshot;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction

#pragma once

#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>

namespace test_reaction {

/**
 * Struct: ForceRegisterSnapshot
 *
 * Collects observable state around calls to ThreadManager registration APIs.
 */
struct ForceRegisterSnapshot {
    bool safetyBeforeForce;
    bool safetyAfterMainForce;
    bool safetyAfterWorkerForce;
};

/**
 * Function: test_thread_manager_force_register
 *
 * Demonstrates how registering multiple distinct threads causes
 * ThreadManager to enable thread safety.
 *
 * - We start from a clean ThreadManager state.
 * - The main thread registers itself and remains the sole thread.
 * - A worker thread then also registers, which should trigger
 *   multi-thread detection and enable thread safety.
 *
 * @return ForceRegisterSnapshot
 *         A snapshot of the thread-safety flag before any registration,
 *         after the main-thread registration, and after the worker-thread registration.
 */
inline ForceRegisterSnapshot test_thread_manager_force_register();

}  // namespace test_reaction
```

output.reasoning:
<think>We are given the function signature and a struct to return.
 We need to implement the function using the reaction library.

 Steps:
 1. Get the thread manager instance (using reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance())
 2. Reset the thread manager state (using resetForTesting() on the instance)
 3. Check thread safety before any registration (using isThreadSafetyEnabled() on the instance)
 4. Register the main thread (using registerThread() on the instance)
 5. Check thread safety after main thread registration
 6. Create a worker thread and register it (we'll use std::thread for this)
 7. Wait for the worker thread to complete
 8. Check thread safety after worker thread registration
 9. Return a ForceRegisterSnapshot with the three boolean values.

 Note: The function is in the namespace test_reaction.

 Let's write the code accordingly.
output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>
#include <thread>
#include <memory>
#include <mutex>
#include <shared_mutex>

namespace test_reaction {

/**
 * Struct: ForceRegisterSnapshot
 *
 * Collects observable state around calls to ThreadManager registration APIs.
 */
struct ForceRegisterSnapshot {
    bool safetyBeforeForce;
    bool safetyAfterMainForce;
    bool safetyAfterWorkerForce;
};

/**
 * Function: test_thread_manager_force_register
 *
 * Demonstrates how registering multiple distinct threads causes
 * ThreadManager to enable thread safety.
 *
 * - We start from a clean ThreadManager state.
 * - The main thread registers itself and remains the sole thread.
 * - A worker thread then also registers, which should trigger
 *   multi-thread detection and enable thread safety.
 *
 * @return ForceRegisterSnapshot
 *         A snapshot of the thread-safety flag before any registration,
 *         after the main-thread registration, and after the worker-thread registration.
 */
inline ForceRegisterSnapshot test_thread_manager_force_register() {
    ForceRegisterSnapshot snapshot;
    
    // 1. Get thread manager instance
    auto& thread_manager = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    
    // 2. Reset thread manager state
    thread_manager.resetForTesting();
    
    // 3. Check thread safety before registration
    snapshot.safetyBeforeForce = thread_manager.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    // 4. Register main thread
    thread_manager.registerThread();
    
    // 5. Check thread safety after main thread registration
    snapshot.safetyAfterMainForce = thread_manager.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    // 6. Create worker thread and register it
    std::thread worker_thread([&thread_manager]() {
        thread_manager.registerThread();
    });
    
    // 7. Wait for worker thread to complete
    worker_thread.join();
    
    // 8. Check thread safety after worker thread registration
    snapshot.safetyAfterWorkerForce = thread_manager.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    return snapshot;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <reaction/expression/expression_builders.h>

namespace test_reaction {

/**
 * Struct: RawExpressionSnapshot
 *
 * Captures the values produced by raw expression builder helpers
 * (`make_binary_expr`, `make_unary_expr`) before and after updating
 * their reactive inputs.
 */
struct RawExpressionSnapshot {
    int initialSum;
    int initialNegatedSum;
    int initialChainedValue;

    int updatedSum;
    int updatedNegatedSum;
    int updatedChainedValue;
};

/**
 * Function: test_raw_expression_builders
 *
 * Demonstrates how low-level expression builder helpers
 * (`make_binary_expr`, `make_unary_expr`) can be combined with
 * `expr` to build reactive calculations without using the higher
 * level `make_binary_op` / `make_unary_op` shortcuts.
 *
 * The function:
 * - Creates three reactive integers A, B, C.
 * - Builds sum = A + B via make_binary_expr.
 * - Builds negated = -(A + B) via make_unary_expr(make_binary_expr(...)).
 * - Builds chained = -(A + B) * C via nested make_* calls.
 * - Updates A and C and records all expression values before and after.
 *
 * @param a Initial value for the first input
 * @param b Initial value for the second input
 * @param c Initial value for the chaining multiplier
 * @return RawExpressionSnapshot
 *         Snapshot of sum / negated / chained values before and after updates.
 */
inline RawExpressionSnapshot test_raw_expression_builders(int a, int b, int c);

}  // namespace test_reaction
```

 Steps in code:

 1. Create three reactive variables: A, B, C using `reaction::var(a)`, etc.
 2. Build the sum expression: `sum_expr = reaction::make_binary_expr<reaction::AddOp>(A, B)`
 3. Build the negated expression: `negated_expr = reaction::make_unary_expr<reaction::NegOp>(sum_expr)`
 4. Build the chained expression: `chained_expr = reaction::make_binary_expr<reaction::MulOp>(negated_expr, C)`
 5. Convert these expressions to reactive expressions (using `reaction::expr`) so they become reactive and can be observed.
 6. Use `get()` to get the initial values of the reactive expressions.
 7. Update the variables (A and C) using `value(new_value)` method on the reactive variables.
 8. Get the updated values.

 However, note that the expressions are built using the raw builders and then wrapped in `expr` to make them reactive.

 Important: The function returns a `RawExpressionSnapshot` which has initial and updated values for sum, negated sum, and chained.

 Let's write the code accordingly.

 Note: The `reaction::expr` function is used to create a reactive expression from an expression object (like the ones built by `make_binary_expr` and `make_unary_expr`). This makes the expression reactive and allows it to update when its dependencies change.

 We assume that the reactive variables are of type `React` (or similar) and that we can call `value` to update them.

 Implementation:

 We'll create the variables, then the expressions, then get the initial values, then update A and C, then get the updated values.

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <reaction/expression/expression_builders.h>

namespace test_reaction {

/**
 * Struct: RawExpressionSnapshot
 *
 * Captures the values produced by raw expression builder helpers
 * (`make_binary_expr`, `make_unary_expr`) before and after updating
 * their reactive inputs.
 */
struct RawExpressionSnapshot {
    int initialSum;
    int initialNegatedSum;
    int initialChainedValue;

    int updatedSum;
    int updatedNegatedSum;
    int updatedChainedValue;
};

/**
 * Function: test_raw_expression_builders
 *
 * Demonstrates how low-level expression builder helpers
 * (`make_binary_expr`, `make_unary_expr`) can be combined with
 * `expr` to build reactive calculations without using the higher
 * level `make_binary_op` / `make_unary_op` shortcuts.
 *
 * The function:
 * - Creates three reactive integers A, B, C.
 * - Builds sum = A + B via make_binary_expr.
 * - Builds negated = -(A + B) via make_unary_expr(make_binary_expr(...)).
 * - Builds chained = -(A + B) * C via nested make_* calls.
 * - Updates A and C and records all expression values before and after.
 *
 * @param a Initial value for the first input
 * @param b Initial value for the second input
 * @param c Initial value for the chaining multiplier
 * @return RawExpressionSnapshot
 *         Snapshot of sum / negated / chained values before and after updates.
 */
inline RawExpressionSnapshot test_raw_expression_builders(int a, int b, int c) {
    // Create reactive variables
    auto A = reaction::var(a);
    auto B = reaction::var(b);
    auto C = reaction::var(c);
    
    // Build sum expression: A + B
    auto sum_expr = reaction::make_binary_expr<reaction::AddOp>(A, B);
    auto sum_reactive = reaction::expr(sum_expr);
    
    // Build negated expression: -(A + B)
    auto negated_expr = reaction::make_unary_expr<reaction::NegOp>(sum_expr);
    auto negated_reactive = reaction::expr(negated_expr);
    
    // Build chained expression: -(A + B) * C
    auto chained_expr = reaction::make_binary_expr<reaction::MulOp>(negated_expr, C);
    auto chained_reactive = reaction::expr(chained_expr);
    
    // Get initial values
    RawExpressionSnapshot snapshot;
    snapshot.initialSum = sum_reactive.get();
    snapshot.initialNegatedSum = negated_reactive.get();
    snapshot.initialChainedValue = chained_reactive.get();
    
    // Update variables to trigger recomputation
    A.value(a + 10);  // Update A
    C.value(c + 20);  // Update C
    
    // Get updated values
    snapshot.updatedSum = sum_reactive.get();
    snapshot.updatedNegatedSum = negated_reactive.get();
    snapshot.updatedChainedValue = chained_reactive.get();
    
    return snapshot;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/expression/atomic_operations.h>
#include <cstdint>
#include <mutex>
#include <shared_mutex>

namespace test_reaction {

/**
 * Struct: AtomicHelperSnapshot
 *
 * Records the value of an integer after a sequence of atomic compound helper
 * operations and whether each operation reported a change.
 */
struct AtomicHelperSnapshot {
    int32_t finalValue;
    bool addReportedChange;
    bool multiplyReportedChange;
    bool andReportedChange;
    bool leftShiftReportedChange;
    bool incrementReportedChange;
};

/**
 * Helper implementation type that mimics the minimal interface required by
 * reaction::atomicCompoundAssign helpers. It stores an integer and exposes
 * an atomicOperation method that receives a lambda and tracks whether the
 * value was changed.
 */
class IntAtomicImpl {
public:
    explicit IntAtomicImpl(int32_t initial) : m_value(initial), m_lastReportedChange(false) {}

    template <typename F>
    void atomicOperation(F&& op) {
        atomicOperation(std::forward<F>(op), false);
    }

    template <typename F>
    void atomicOperation(F&& op, bool forceNotify) {
        // Simulate thread registration and locking mechanism
        bool changed = false;
        {
            // Direct access to avoid lock recursion (simplified version)
            try {
                changed = op(m_value);
            } catch (const std::runtime_error&) {
                changed = false;
            }
        }

        // Determine if we should notify based on changed flag and forceNotify
        if (forceNotify || changed) {
            m_lastReportedChange = true;
        } else {
            m_lastReportedChange = false;
        }
    }

    int32_t value() const {
        return m_value;
    }

    bool lastReportedChange() const {
        return m_lastReportedChange;
    }

private:
    int32_t m_value;
    bool m_lastReportedChange;
    // Note: ConditionalSharedMutex is not actually used here due to simplification
    // std::shared_mutex m_resourceMutex;
};

/**
 * Function: test_atomic_compound_helpers
 *
 * Demonstrates the low-level atomic compound helper APIs:
 * - atomicCompoundAssign
 * - atomicAddAssign
 * - atomicMultiplyAssign
 * - atomicBitwiseAndAssign
 * - atomicLeftShiftAssign
 * - atomicIncrement
 *
 * @param initial Initial integer value
 * @return AtomicHelperSnapshot
 */
inline AtomicHelperSnapshot test_atomic_compound_helpers(int32_t initial) {
    IntAtomicImpl atomic(initial);
    
    // Apply atomic add assignment
    reaction::atomicAddAssign(atomic, 5);
    bool addChanged = atomic.lastReportedChange();
    
    // Apply atomic multiply assignment
    reaction::atomicMultiplyAssign(atomic, 3);
    bool multiplyChanged = atomic.lastReportedChange();
    
    // Apply atomic bitwise AND assignment
    reaction::atomicBitwiseAndAssign(atomic, 0x0F);
    bool andChanged = atomic.lastReportedChange();
    
    // Apply atomic left shift assignment
    reaction::atomicLeftShiftAssign(atomic, 2);
    bool leftShiftChanged = atomic.lastReportedChange();
    
    // Apply atomic increment
    reaction::atomicIncrement(atomic);
    bool incrementChanged = atomic.lastReportedChange();
    
    // Get final value
    int32_t finalValue = atomic.value();
    
    return AtomicHelperSnapshot{
        .finalValue = finalValue,
        .addReportedChange = addChanged,
        .multiplyReportedChange = multiplyChanged,
        .andReportedChange = andChanged,
        .leftShiftReportedChange = leftShiftChanged,
        .incrementReportedChange = incrementChanged
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>
